coredata.dc:title: A comprehensive understanding on the roles of carbon dots in metallated graphyne based catalyst for photoinduced H2O2 production
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yajie
Xu, Linli
Wang, Xiao
Wang, Zhenzhen
Liu, Yan
Wang, Yang
Wang, Qiaolian
Wang, Zitong
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Wong, Wai-Yeung
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalysis is regarded as a sustainable H2O2 manufacturing method. Carbon dots (CDs) are widely used in photocatalysis due to their excellent photoelectric features, while the current understanding of their functions is incomplete. Metallated graphyne is raised as a novel photofunctional 2D organometallic nanosheet which combines the merits of metals and graphynes. To deeply understand the functions of CDs and unlock the potential of mercurated graphyne (HgL1) in photocatalysis, a CDs/HgL1 composite catalyst was constructed. Under visible light, the H2O2 production rate catalyzed by CDs/HgL1 (800 µmol h−1 g−1) is 6.4 times higher than that of pristine HgL1 bulk (125 µmol h−1 g−1). The effects of CDs on HgL1 run through the photocatalytic process, including accelerating the electron-hole separation and migration, improving the redox activity and changing the surface state from hydrophobicity to hydrophilicity. This work offers new avenues for the intelligent design of CDs-based photocatalysts and pioneers the application of metallated graphyne in photocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Metallated graphyne
Photocatalysis
Hydrogen peroxide
Transient photovoltage

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structure of the photocatalysts
Pathway and mechanism of photocatalytic H2O2 production

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM, HR-TEM images and size distribution map of CDs. (b) TEM and (c) HR-TEM images of CDs/HgL1. (d) HAADF-STEM and the elemental mapping images for (e) C, (f) N, and (g) Hg elements of CDs/HgL1.

(a) XRD patterns of HgL1 and CDs/HgL1. (b) FTIR spectra of CDs, HgL1 and CDs/HgL1. (c) XPS survey and (d) C 1 s spectra of HgL1 and CDs/HgL1. (e) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra of HgL1 and CDs/HgL1. (f) The band structure diagram of pristine HgL1.
(a) TPR and (b) EIS of HgL1 and CDs/HgL1 measured in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution. (c) PL spectra and (d) TRPL spectra of HgL1 and CDs/HgL1 composite.

TPV measurements of pristine HgL1 and CDs/HgL1. Comparison of (a) TPV curves, (b) maximum extraction rate (t
max ) of charge, (c) maximum charge extraction efficiency (A) and (d) electron attenuation constants (τ) of pristine HgL1 and CDs/HgL1. (e) Surface effective electron number (n
e
=A*τ/t
max ) calculated. The contact angles of (f) pristine HgL1 and (g) CDs/HgL1.

(a) Time dependence of H2O2 production catalyzed by CDs, pristine HgL1 and CDs/HgL1. (b) Cycling stability of CDs/HgL1. (c) Comparison of H2O2 production of photocatalysts with different content of CDs. (d) AQE values versus the UV–vis spectrum of CDs/HgL1.

(a) Comparison of H2O2 production catalyzed by CDs/HgL1 (10 mg) for 4 h under different reaction environments. (b) Comparison of H2O2 production catalyzed by CDs/HgL1 (10 mg) for 4 h with different sacrificial agents. (c-d) DMPO spin-trapping EPR spectra of CDs/HgL1 for ·O2 - and ·OH. RRDE measurements of CDs/HgL1 for (e) ORR and (f) WOR in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution (10 mV s−1, 1600 rpm).

In-situ TPV tests of pristine HgL1, CDs/HgL1 and CDs. The in-situ TPV curves of (a) pristine HgL1, (c) CDs/HgL1 and (e) CDs under N2/O2 saturated MeCN solution. The in-situ TPV curves of (b) pristine HgL1, (d) CDs/HgL1 and (f) CDs under N2 saturated MeCN solution and 0.5 vol% H2O/MeCN (v/v) solution.
(a) Mechanism diagram of photoinduced H2O2 production catalyzed by CDs/HgL1. (b) The roles of CDs in CDs/HgL1 photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: A comprehensive understanding on the roles of carbon dots in metallated graphyne based catalyst for photoinduced H2O2 production

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots/mecurated graphyne (HgL1) composite catalyst constructed for efficient photoinduced H2O2 production.

Photocatalysis is regarded as a sustainable H2O2 manufacturing method. Carbon dots (CDs) are widely used in photocatalysis due to their excellent photoelectric features, while the current understanding of their functions is incomplete. Metallated graphyne is raised as a novel photofunctional 2D organometallic nanosheet which combines the merits of metals and graphynes. To deeply understand the functions of CDs and unlock the potential of mercurated graphyne (HgL1) in photocatalysis, a CDs/HgL1 composite catalyst was constructed. Under visible light, the H2O2 production rate catalyzed by CDs/HgL1 (800 µmol h−1 g−1) is 6.4 times higher than that of pristine HgL1 bulk (125 µmol h−1 g−1). The effects of CDs on HgL1 run through the photocatalytic process, including accelerating the electron-hole separation and migration, improving the redox activity and changing the surface state from hydrophobicity to hydrophilicity. This work offers new avenues for the intelligent design of CDs-based photocatalysts and pioneers the application of metallated graphyne in photocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is gaining popularity as a viable new liquid fuel and ecologically friendly oxidant for chemical synthesis and environmental remediation [1]. The anthraquinone (AQ) oxidation process is the principal source of H2O2, which unfortunately consumes fossil fuels and produces toxic waste [2]. Photocatalysis, which utilizes water and oxygen as raw materials and solar energy, is seen to be a sustainable and promising process for H2O2 production [3,4].

Carbon dots (CDs) typically are carbon nanoparticles less than 10 nm in size with various surface functional groups [5]. Due to their excellent optical and electrical properties, CDs have been extensively used in the field of photocatalytic pollutant degradation, hydrogen production, CO2 reduction, and organic synthesis [6–8]. The current understanding of the function of CDs focuses mostly on light absorption, electron-hole generation, separation, migration and surface chemical reactions. CDs have flexible functions, while there is still a lack of the comprehensive understanding on their roles in photocatalysis [9].

Two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials have received much attention due to their remarkable electronic and optical properties [10–13]. In recent years, graphene [14], carbon nitride [15,16], MXenes [17] and their self-assembled 2D nanocomposites [18–21] have made great progress in the field of energy conversion. Metallated graphyne, which integrates both the advantages of metal center and graphyne, is considered as a promising material. In the previous work, we have reported the first experimental preparation of free-standing mercurated graphyne nanosheets (pristine HgL1, Fig. S1) [22]. HgL1 has a suitable band structure and strong light absorption, but rapid carrier recombination and hydrophobic surface limit its development in photocatalytic applications in water.

CDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method. Citric acid (CA, 1.0507 g) and ethylenediamine (EDA, 355 μL) were dissolved in 10 mL water. Then the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and placed in an oven at 200 ºC for 8 h. The obtained solution was filtered with 0.22 µm filter and dialyzed in deionized water for 24 h. Finally, the red-brown CDs powder was obtained by the freeze-drying method.

HgL1 bulk was obtained according to the previous work [22]. The CDs/HgL1 composite was prepared by the hydrothermal method. HgL1 (50 mg) and CDs (0, 0.1, 1, 5, 10, 20 wt%) were dispersed and ultrasonicated in 10 mL ethanol. Then the aforementioned mixture was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 100 ºC for 12 h. After centrifugation, washing and drying, the product CDs/HgL1 was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of the photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image in Fig. 1a shows that CDs are uniformly dispersed spheres with a size of< 3 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image presents the lattice spacing of CDs is 0.21 nm, which matches the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [23]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern depicted in Fig. S2a exhibits a broad peak at about 26°, which originated from the (002) lattice of graphite carbon [24]. The Raman spectra of the CDs in Fig. S2b exhibit two peaks at 1328 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1, belonging to the D and G peaks [25]. Furthermore, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of CDs provided in Fig. S2c indicates that the surface has abundant oxygen-containing groups (O–H, CO, C–O–C). Moreover, N–H and C–N bonds were observed at 3259 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 [26,27]. The element composition and bonding mode of CDs were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). From the XPS survey spectrum in Fig. S3, CDs consist of three elements, including carbon (65.6%), nitrogen (13.5%) and oxygen (20.9%), respectively. The C 1 s spectrum indicates the presence of C–C, C–N, C–O and CO bonds at 284.4, 285.2, 286.1 and 287.6 eV, respectively [28]. There are three peaks in the N 1 s spectra at 399.4, 400.2 and 401.5 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively [29].

The morphology of CDs/HgL1 was observed by TEM and HR-TEM. Fig. 1b displays the TEM image of CDs/HgL1. HgL1, as a new 2D metallated graphyne material, shows a sheet and layered structure [22]. The TEM image in Fig. 1c shows the distribution of CDs on HgL1, and the corresponding lattice fringes of both can be observed from the inset HR-TEM images. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm belongs to the (100) crystal plane of CDs, and that of 0.34 nm corresponds to the interlayer distance of HgL1. The element mapping graphs in Figs. 1d-g reflect that C, N, and Hg elements are distributed uniformly on CDs/HgL1.

Next, the crystal structure was studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As presented in Fig. 2a, each of HgL1 and CDs/HgL1 has four diffraction peaks at 6.4°, 12.8°, 20.0° and 25.8°, assigning to (100), (200), (300) and (001) facets, respectively [22]. In addition, the characteristic peak of CDs is not observed in CDs/HgL1 because it overlaps with that of HgL1. As can be seen from the FTIR spectra in Fig. 2b, as compared with HgL1, CDs/HgL1 shows no significant IR spectral change in the functional groups except that the characteristic peak intensity of O-H groups present in CDs increases within 3200–3500 cm−1 [26]. In addition, XPS spectra further reveal the elemental composition and chemical bonding of CDs/HgL1. The XPS survey spectrum presents that the oxygen content of CDs/HgL1 increases obviously (Fig. 2c, Table S1), and the C 1 s spectrum of CDs/HgL1 presents a new peak at 288.2 eV, which may be related to the CO bond introduced by CDs (Fig. 2d) [28]. Besides, three peaks at 283.8, 284.4 and 285.4 eV are attributed to the C–Hg, C–C and C–N bonds, respectively [22].

Fig. 2e presents the UV–vis absorption properties. It can be seen that pristine HgL1 has a good light-harvesting capacity at the wavelength shorter than 500 nm. After being modified by CDs, the visible light-harvest ability is improved. The corresponding photographs of pristine HgL1 and CDs/HgL1 are presented in Fig. S4a-S4b. The bandgap of HgL1 is 2.63 eV calculated from the Tauc plot (Fig. S5a) [30]. In addition, the flat band potential (EFB) of HgL1 was studied by the Mott-Schottky (M-S) plot. As shown in Fig. S5b, HgL1 shows a positive slope, indicating the n-type feature of semiconductors. For n-type semiconductors, EFB is approximately equal to the conduction band potential (ECB) [31]. The flat band position of HgL1 is − 0.60 V (vs. RHE) based on the M-S analysis. Thus, the ECB of HgL1 is − 0.6 V. Moreover, the potential of valance band (EVB) can be evaluated to be 2.03 V based on the formula EVB = ECB + Eg [32]. Fig. 2f depicts the energy level diagram of HgL1, indicating that its band structure can meet the potentials of ORR and water oxidation reaction (WOR) at the same time.

The transient photocurrent response (TPR) curves of HgL1 and CDs/HgL1 are shown in Fig. 3a. Compared with the pristine HgL1, the CDs/HgL1 composite has a higher photocurrent, corresponding to a higher electron-hole separation efficiency [33,34]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was applied to investigate the internal resistance of charge transfer (Rct). CDs/HgL1 has a smaller Rct to HgL1 (Fig. 3b), indicating that CDs can speed up the charge transfer [34,35]. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of HgL1 and CDs/HgL1 in Fig. 3c indicate that pristine HgL1 exhibits strong fluorescence intensity at 523 nm due to severe carrier recombination [36]. In contrast, obvious fluorescence quenching is observed in CDs/HgL1. The distinctly suppressed radiative recombination in CDs/HgL1 shows that charge transfer from HgL1 to CDs occurs [37]. To quantitatively understand the photo-generated charge carrier recombination rate, TRPL spectra were obtained by a picosecond pulsed laser with an excitation of 470 nm (emission peak at 553 nm). As provided in Fig. 3d, the average PL lifetime increased from 20.5 ns (pristine HgL1) to 35.8 ns (CDs/HgL1). The prolongation of PL lifetime and the corresponding inhibition of electron-hole recombination suggest the enhanced charge separation efficiency, which is beneficial for photocatalytic activities.

To explore the interfacial charge transfer kinetics of these catalysts, TPV tests were performed. Fig. 4a exhibits that CDs/HgL1 has a higher photocurrent intensity than that of pristine HgL1, corresponding to the higher electron-hole separation efficiency [38]. After magnifying analysis and area integration of the original TPV data, it can be analyzed that the charge extraction rate (t
max1 ) and maximum charge extraction efficiency (A) of CDs/HgL1 are improved (Figs. 4b-4c, Table S2), corresponding to a faster charge transfer rate and more photogenerated charge on the surface [39]. Besides, the electron attenuation constant (τ) of CDs/HgL1 is also improved slightly (Fig. 4d). Moreover, the surface effective electron numbers (n
e
= A*τ/t
max ) of the catalysts are calculated and listed in Fig. 4e. It can be seen that the n
e of CDs/HgL1 (n
e
= 0.162) is improved compared with that of pristine HgL1 (n
e = 0.140), indicating that CDs/HgL1 has a stronger photocatalytic ability [40].

It is worth noting that CDs not only improve the electron-hole separation efficiency of HgL1 but also regulate the surface hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties. As depicted in Fig. 4f, the contact angle of pristine HgL1 is 145.4°, indicating the surface is hydrophobic. Because of the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on CDs, the synthesized CDs/HgL1 catalyst shows a hydrophilic surface in which the contact angle is 59.8° (Fig. 4g).

The photocatalytic properties of pristine HgL1 and CDs/HgL1 were explored. No hydrogen production was detected no matter whether HgL1 or CDs/HgL1 was used as the photocatalyst as shown in Fig. S6. The concentration of the produced H2O2 was determined by the titanium sulfate method, and the corresponding curve was placed in Fig. S7. The time dependence curves of H2O2 production catalyzed by CDs, pristine HgL1 and CDs/HgL1 are displayed in Fig. 5a. None of them produces H2O2 under dark conditions. It is noticed that no H2O2 was detected in the system catalyzed by pure CDs, indicating CDs can not directly reduce O2 to H2O2 in water under visible light irradiation. For CDs/HgL1, at the initial stage of the reaction, it produces H2O2 at a rate of 800 µmol h−1 g−1, which is 6.4 times that of pristine HgL1 (125 µmol h−1 g−1). After 12 h, the H2O2 concentration almost remains unchanged due to the equilibrium of H2O2 production and decomposition, and the highest H2O2 concentration at equilibrium can reach at about 5 mmol L−1 [41]. The re-usability of CDs/HgL1 was studied by a cyclic stability experiment. As exhibited in Fig. 5b, CDs/HgL1 shows a stable and high catalytic efficiency in five cycles. The TEM image of CDs/HgL1 is shown after five cycles in Fig. S8, which shows almost no any change compared with that before reaction, indicating its good morphological stability. Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks of CDs/HgL1 after reaction in the XRD pattern and FTIR spectrum still can be observed in Fig. S9a-S9b, signifying that the structure is stable. Furthermore, the XPS spectra of CDs/HgL1 in Fig. S10 show no difference from those before the reaction, indicating that its chemical composition has not changed. As a result, CDs/HgL1 shows good morphology and structural stability in five cycles. The effect of CDs content on the catalytic performance was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5c, with the increase of CDs content, the catalytic performance is improved firstly, then declines slightly at above 10 wt%. The optimal catalytic performance was realized when 10 wt% CDs were used in the synthesis of CDs/HgL1. The declined catalytic efficiency with more than 10 wt% CDs might be caused by the inner filter effect [42,43], which means the excess CDs compete with HgL1 in light absorption, hence decreasing the photocatalytic performance. Apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) is a vital index to evaluate the light utilization efficiency of photocatalysts [44]. The results in Fig. 5d reveal that the variation trend of AQE at different wavelengths is consistent with the light-absorption spectrum. The AQE values can be calculated to be 0.70% and 0.66% at 365 nm and 420 nm, respectively.

Even though Hg is usually recognized as a damaging element to the environment, HgL1 nanosheets have been shown to exhibit low cytotoxicity and no severe toxic impact on the human body [22]. Meanwhile, the TGA curve in Fig. S11 demonstrates the onset decomposition temperature of CDs/HgL1 is about 260 ºC, revealing it has good thermal stability. In addition, the catalyst can be separated from the H2O2 solution using a 0.22 µm membrane. More importantly, according to Table S3, no Hg was detected by the inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) test in the solution after reaction during the time dependence test of H2O2 production and cyclic stability test. Therefore, CDs/HgL1 can serve as a safe and efficient photocatalytic catalyst for the H2O2 production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pathway and mechanism of photocatalytic H2O2 production
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the reaction pathway of H2O2 production catalyzed by CDs/HgL1, photocatalytic experiments under different atmospheric conditions were carried out [45]. As presented in Fig. 6a, the production rate of H2O2 measured in the O2 saturated system is slightly higher than that in the air condition, implying that O2 is an imperative reactant. However, almost no H2O2 is produced in the N2 system, indicating that CDs/HgL1 oxidizes water to O2 rather than H2O2. Therefore, H2O2 was produced through an ORR. Active species trapping experiments further confirmed the reaction pathway (Fig. 6b) [46]. AgNO3 and EDTA-2Na act as the electron (e-) and hole (h+) scavengers. The production decreased sharply when electrons were captured, while they increased slightly when holes were captured. The results indicate that H2O2 generation is an ORR-dominated process, and the trapping of holes is conducive to more electrons participating in ORR. Besides, BQ and TBA were added to capture superoxide radical (·O2 -) and hydroxyl radical (·OH). In the BQ-containing system, only a small amount of H2O2 was detected, illustrating that the ·O2 - is an essential component during the process of photocatalytic H2O2 production. TBA has no negative effect on H2O2 production, implying that ·OH did not participate in H2O2 production. Furthermore, DMPO spin-trapping EPR tests were performed to detect the presence of ·O2 - and ·OH [47]. As shown in Figs. 6c-6d, ·O2 - was detected under light condition while ·OH was not (·O2 - or ·OH was not detected in dark), which is consistent with the above experimental results. As a result, H2O2 was generated via a two-electron ORR process.

Furthermore, the pathway of H2O2 production also can be verified by rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) measurements [48]. The potential of Pt ring electrode was set at 0.9 V to detect the H2O2 generated on the disk electrode. Fig. 6e shows that CDs/HgL1 has the ability of oxygen reduction (solid line). Meanwhile, an oxidation current (dash line) was observed on the Pt ring electrode, indicating that CDs/HgL1 could reduce O2 to H2O2. In Fig. 6f, the increasing disk current (solid line) with potentials higher than 1.6 V reveals that water oxidation occurs on the disk electrode covered with CDs/HgL1. However, no oxidation current (dash line) was observed on the ring electrode, which means no H2O2 was generated in the water oxidation reaction (WOR). Hence, the RRDE measurements conclude that H2O2 is produced by ORR occurred on CDs/HgL1 rather than WOR.

To gain a deep understanding of the interface electron transfer relationship between CDs and pristine HgL1, in-situ TPV measurements under different conditions were conducted [37,49]. The charge consumed (∆Q) by the redox reactions can be tracked by calculating the integral area difference of curves under different conditions [38,50]. As displayed in Fig. 7a, the photocurrent intensity of HgL1 in O2 saturated MeCN is lower than that of N2 saturated MeCN solution (ΔQ1 = 0.051), illustrating that electrons were consumed by ORR. The increased charge integral area (ΔQ2 = 0.029) in Fig. 7b is attributed to the depletion of holes by WOR, resulting in more electrons being detected. Similarly, as shown in Figs. 7c-7d, the charge consumed by ORR (ΔQ3 = 0.137) and WOR (ΔQ4 = 0.068) on CDs/HgL1 was calculated. The percentage of charge consumed (∆Q) in O2 saturated MeCN solution/N2 saturated 0.5 vol% H2O/MeCN (v/v) solution to the integral area in N2 saturated MeCN solution represents the ORR/WOR ability of the photocatalyst. It can be seen from Table S4 that, compared with WOR (9.6%), ORR capability has been improved to a greater extent (21.7%). Meanwhile, Figs. 7e-7f suggests that CDs can reduce oxygen well (ΔQ5 = 0.210) but have no response to water oxidation (ΔQ6 ≈ 0), indicating that CDs act as the center of ORR to promote the catalytic reaction. At the same time, the WOR rate also has been promoted due to the more electron consumption and the increased surface hydrophilicity. To reveal the active sites of this reaction, electrochemical ORR and OER tests were carried out. As shown in Fig. S12, the ORR performance of CDs is better than that of HgL1 but the OER performance is inferior to that of HgL1, indicating that CDs are the main active sites of ORR and HgL1 is that of WOR.

In order to find the key factors for improving the present catalyst performance, the ratios of n
e , ORR activity and H2O2 production rate were calculated (Table S5). The rate of H2O2 production by CDs/HgL1 is 6.4 times that of pristine HgL1, while n
e is only 1.16. Although CDs can accelerate the carrier separation and migration, n
e is not the primary factor to affect the H2O2 production in the present catalyst system. The in-situ TPV test verifies that the ORR ability of CDs/HgL1 is 2.53 times of pristine HgL1. However, this enhanced ORR activity is also not the factor to determine the catalytic activity. Now, let us look back to the hydrophobic surface of pristine HgL1. The comparison of pristine HgL1 and CDs/HgL1 dispersed in water was shown in Fig. S13a-S13b. The pristine HgL1 only floats on the surface of water, whereas CDs/HgL1 disperses uniformly. It suggested that CDs/HgL1 can be better wetted by the aqueous solution, making the reactants more accessible to the active site, which is conducive to the photocatalytic reaction [51]. All the above results suggested that CDs promote the photocatalytic H2O2 production of HgL1 by improving the n
e , ORR activity and changing the catalyst surface from hydrophobic to hydrophilic. Based on the above results, we proposed a possible mechanism of H2O2 production catalyzed by CDs/HgL1 photocatalyst ( Fig. 8a). Under visible light irradiation, the photo-generated electrons on HgL1 can migrate rapidly to CDs for ORR, and the remaining holes on its VB participate in the WOR. Although HgL1 has a suitable band structure, its surface hydrophobicity and rapid carriers recombination are unfavorable for ORR and WOR in pure water systems. CDs, which act as the centers for capturing and transferring electrons, can accelerate the carrier separation and migration, as well as improve the ORR activity. In this system, CDs also alter the catalyst surface from hydrophobic to hydrophilic, which is conducive to the reactions that follow (Fig. 8b). The synergistic effects of higher n
e , improved the ORR activity and hydrophilic surface jointly realize the efficient photoinduced production of H2O2 by the CDs/HgL1 catalyst.

In this work, the roles of CDs in photocatalysis were studied based on the photocatalysts of CDs/HgL1 composite. Under visible light, the H2O2 production rate catalyzed by CDs/HgL1 is 6.4 times that of pristine HgL1. TPV tests reveals CDs can accelerate the electron-hole separation and migration, resulting in higher n
e . In-situ TPV test confirmed CDs as the ORR reactive sites can improve the ORR activity. Moreover, CDs alter the catalyst surface from hydrophobic to hydrophilic, facilitating the construction of a three-phase interface. In short, the functions of CDs show up in the whole photocatalytic process: accelerating the electron-hole separation and migration, improving the redox activity and changing the surface state from hydrophobicity to hydrophilicity. This work proposes a novel strategy for the intelligent design of CDs-based photocatalysts and opens up the application of metallated graphyne in photocatalysis.

Yajie Zhao: Data curation, Formal Analysis. Linli Xu: Synthesis and formal Analysis, Supervision. Zhenzhen Wang: Methodology. Xiao Wang: Software. Yan Liu: Validation. Yang Wang: Visualization. Qiaolian Wang: Investigation. Zitong Wang: Investigation. Hui Huang: Formal Analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Wai-Yeung Wong: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhenhui Kang: Conceptualization, Project Administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program of China (2018YFE0306105), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2020YFA0406104, 2020YFA0406101), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), the Science, Technology and Innovation Committee of Shenzhen Municipality (JCYJ20180507183413211), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51725204, 21771132, 21905241, 52073242, 51972216, 52041202‬), The Hong Kong Polytechnic University (1-BD64, 1-ZE1C), the RGC Senior Research Fellowship Scheme (SRFS2021–5S01), the Hong Kong Research Grants Council (PolyU 15307321), Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Joint Laboratory for Photonic-Thermal-Electrical Energy Materials and Devices (GDSTC No. 2019B121205001), Research Institute for Smart Energy (RISE), the Endowed Professorship in Energy from Miss Clarea Au (847 S), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041), Key-Area Research and Development Program of Guangdong Province (2019B010933001), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the 111 Project, and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nantod.2022.101428.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yajie Zhao is currently a Ph.D. student in Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM) of Soochow University, PR China. Since 2020, she has been conducting her Ph.D. work in FUNSOM under the supervision of Prof. Zhenhui Kang. Her current research interests mainly focus on the design, synthesis and application of polymer-based catalysts in photocatalysis.

Linli Xu obtained her Ph.D. degree from Guangzhou Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) in 2010. She then carried out research at Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, CAS, from 2012 to 2017. Then she worked at the Hong Kong Polytechnic University (PolyU) as a research fellow in the group of Prof. Wai-Yeung Wong from 2017. Now she is a research assistant professor in PolyU from 2021 and her research focuses on 2D carbon and carbon-rich materials and polymer materials.

Zhenzhen Wang is currently a Ph.D. student in Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM) of Soochow University, PR China. Since 2020, she has been conducting her Ph.D. work in FUNSOM under the supervision of Prof. Zhenhui Kang. Her current research interests are carbon-based materials utilized in photothermal water evaporation and photocatalysis.

Xiao Wang is in Ph.D. study under the supervision of Prof. Zhenhui Kang in Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM) of Soochow University, PR China. His research interests mainly focus on the design and synthesis of carbon dots and their applications in catalysis and light-emitting diodes.

Yan Liu is currently a Ph.D. student in Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM) of Soochow University, PR China. Since 2020, she has been conducting her Ph.D. work in FUNSOM under the supervision of Prof. Zhenhui Kang and Yang Liu. Her research interests focus on the development of non-noble metal materials for photocatalysis and photoelectric catalysis.

Yang Wang obtained his B.Sc. degree from Anhui University in 2014 and his Ph.D. degree at Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry of Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS). Then he worked at the Hong Kong Polytechnic University (PolyU) as a postdoctoral fellow in the group of Prof. Wai-Yeung Wong from 2020. His research interests mainly focus on artificial photosynthesis, including water splitting, carbon dioxide reduction.

Qiaolian Wang received her B.Sc. and M.Sc. degree from the Department of Material Science and Engineering, Shandong University (SDU) in 2017 and 2020. She is currently a Ph.D. student in the Department of Applied Biology and Chemical Technology, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University under the guidance of Prof. Wai-Yeung Wong. Her research interest focuses on design and development of metallated graphynes of two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials for photoelectrocatalytic applications.

Zitong Wang obtained his B.Sc. (Hons.) degree from the Hong Kong Polytechnic University and worked as an undergraduate researcher in Prof. Wai-Yeung Wong’s group, focusing on the design and synthesis of metal complexes and polymers for potential energy-related applications. He is now pursuing his Ph.D. degree in the University of Chicago.

Hui Huang is currently an associate professor in Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM) and the Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices in Soochow University, PR China. She got her Ph.D. in 2013 at Soochow University. Her research interests include design and synthesis of composite nanomaterials based on carbon, metal, semiconductor and polyoxometallate as well as their application.

Yang Liu is a professor at the Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM) and the Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, PR China. Her main research field is dedicated to the synthesis and assembly of morphologically unique nanostructures and nanocomposites, and their applications in catalysis and energy conversion.

Wai-Yeung Wong obtained his B.Sc. and Ph.D. degrees from the University of Hong Kong. After postdoctoral works at Texas A&M University and the University of Cambridge, he joined Hong Kong Baptist University from 1998 to 2016 and now works at the Hong Kong Polytechnic University as Chair Professor of Chemical Technology and Dean of Faculty of Applied Science and Textiles. Among his awards are the RSC Chemistry of the Transition Metals Award, FACS Distinguished Young Chemist Award, State Natural Science Award from China and RGC Senior Research Fellow Award. His research focuses on synthetic inorganic/organometallic chemistry, aiming at developing photofunctional metal–organic molecules and polymers with energy functions.

Zhenhui Kang is currently a Professor in FUNSOM, Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, PR China. He is the winner of the National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars. His main research interests are in the fields of synthetic and catalytic chemistry of nanomaterials, polyoxometalates, and quantum sized functional materials, as well as the exploring of their applications in nanocatalysis, new energy, and bio-imaging.
